metadata.seed: Well, kind of. It's kind of related to that. So I'm in school, and every few weeks we have, like, papers to write, and I know, I know it. I know I can do it. It's just actually sitting down and doing it.

patient.query: Well, kind of. It's kind of related to that. So I'm in school, and every few weeks we have, like, papers to write, and I know, I know it. I know I can do it. It's just actually sitting down and doing it.

entailment: Able to remember new motor skills (finding)
Ability to remember new motor skills (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re confident in your ability to write the papers, yet something seems to be blocking you from actually getting started. What thoughts or feelings arise when you think about sitting down to write? What small step might help you ease into the task?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel like I know what I’m doing when it comes to writing. But then, when I actually sit down to do it, I just... freeze up? It’s like this wave of anxiety hits me, and I start to think about how it has to be perfect. I feel like if I don’t get it right the first time, then what's the point? I don't know, maybe just breaking it down into smaller sections could help? But even that feels daunting

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like the pressure to get everything perfect is really overwhelming when you actually sit down to write.  
What evidence do you have that if you don’t get it right the first time, there’s no point at all?  
Could you try treating a first draft as a rough sketch—just getting ideas down—rather than a finished piece?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel overwhelming. I think part of me just believes that if it's not good right away, then I’m failing, you know? It’s hard to shake that feeling. Treating the first draft as just a sketch sounds easier, but I still worry about what people will think if it’s not good enough. I guess I’m just scared of showing something I’m not fully proud of.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to get everything perfect on the first try. What evidence do you have that a draft that isn’t flawless means you’re a failure? Maybe you could view the first version as a stepping‑stone—just a place to gather ideas—rather than a final judgment of your worth.

patient.query: I don’t know, it just feels like if I don’t impress right away, then people are going to think I’m not capable or something. Like, I’ve always struggled to keep up with everyone else, so when I actually share something, I want it to show that I can do it. It feels really personal, and I guess I worry that people will see me differently if what I turn in isn't great.

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of pressure to prove yourself right away. What evidence do you have that people will judge you if your work isn’t perfect? Could there be other ways people might interpret your effort?

patient.query: Yeah, I suppose I just assume that people will focus on the flaws. It’s like, I’ve always been afraid of being compared to others. I mean, I’ve heard people talk about their first drafts and how they can be rough, but I don’t know, maybe I just think they have more talent or something? I guess it’s hard to see things from their perspective. I just get stuck in my head about all the ways I might let people down.

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how anxious you feel about being judged and worried that people will focus on your flaws. What evidence do you have that others actually notice or care about those imperfections? Have there been moments when people responded kindly or seemed to overlook those concerns? It might help to consider that most people are preoccupied with their own worries, so the focus on your work may be less intense than it feels.

patient.query: I mean, yeah, I guess I’ve had a few moments where people seemed to appreciate what I wrote. But when I’m in the moment of creating, it’s like those thoughts just vanish. I can’t help but feel like every little mistake is glaring. I know logically that everyone has their own stuff going on, but in those moments, it just feels like all eyes are on me. I don’t know... maybe it’s just hard to let go of that pressure.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like the pressure of writing feels very intense for you.  
What evidence do you have that everyone is watching you and judging your work?  
Can you recall a time when you felt less pressure or noticed that the criticism wasn’t as obvious?

patient.query: Yeah, the pressure can feel really heavy. I mean, I can't point to something specific that shows everyone is watching, it’s more like this nagging feeling that they are, you know? It just feels like everyone is waiting to see if I’ll mess up. But, um, there have been times when I've shared something and people were actually encouraging. Like in group projects, sometimes my ideas got picked, and that felt good. But then I just end up thinking it was luck or that

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of pressure from others, even when you don’t have concrete evidence of being watched. What moments make you feel that everyone is waiting to see if you’ll mess up? And when your ideas are chosen, what thoughts come up that make you think it was just luck? Exploring those thoughts might help you see other ways to interpret the situation.

patient.query: Yeah, there are definitely times in class when I speak up, and I just feel that weight of expectation. It’s like I can hear my heart racing, and I’m convinced that everyone’s silently judging my every word. When my ideas get picked, I often think they just liked it because it was the easiest choice or they felt sorry for me. It’s frustrating, I guess, because I want to feel like I earned it, you know? But instead, I end up downplaying it

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure when you speak up, and that can make it hard to see your own worth. What evidence do you have that people liked your idea because it was easy or out of pity, rather than because of what you actually said? Have there been moments when your classmates or teacher praised the content or originality of your contribution? Exploring those moments might help you feel more confident that your ideas are valued for their own merit.

patient.query: I don’t know, I guess I just... it feels easier to think they chose it because they didn’t want to hurt my feelings. I mean, I do remember a couple of times when someone gave me a compliment about my idea, but those moments feel like exceptions. I guess I just latch onto the fear of being seen as inadequate, and it drowns out any praise. It’s like I’m waiting for someone to call me out or say it was a mistake to pick me.

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding onto the idea that people pick you mainly to spare their own feelings, and that belief can feel very heavy. What evidence do you have that this is the main reason, and can you think of other possible explanations for why they might have chosen you? When you notice those moments of praise, how might they fit into a broader picture of how people view your ideas?

patient.query: I mean, that’s a good point, but it’s hard to shake that feeling. I guess part of me just thinks that if they picked me, it must be because they didn’t want to hurt my feelings or needed a safe choice. Sometimes, when I look back, I realize people might have genuinely liked what I contributed, but it never sticks with me. It's like every time I think of a compliment, I just push it aside. I get caught up in that cycle of doubt,

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding onto the idea that being chosen was more about protecting others than about your own value. What evidence do you have that people truly appreciated what you brought to the table? If you could imagine a scenario where someone genuinely liked your contribution, how might that change the way you view those compliments?
